chapters.title: Having an orthodox method of doing things doesn't encourage creativity
chapters.text:
Principles and methods are essential to every organization, but it gets difficult to be creative once they become too rigid. The ability to be innovative is heavily influenced by the kind of environment one is in. If we are in a tense and hostile environment, we'll pay more attention to perfection than creativity, and being overly cautious limits our innovative ways, making it difficult to express ourselves as we would have loved to.

The best work environments exist in organizations where true personality is encouraged through humor and friendliness. Once people are happy to go to work, they'll have more enthusiasm to make things happen. Humor is a powerful tool for perfecting work-life balance; it gives people a sense of belonging, especially with employees who have a good relationship with their leaders.

Leaders who do not encourage friendship in workplaces will find it difficult to get a team to work together.

When there's a relaxed atmosphere, it's easy to establish unity and discipline. An earned respect is better than a forced one. When people fear you, they'll only do what you want, not what's best for you or the organization. However, if, as a leader, you have a good rapport with your employees, they'll always find ways to please you by being more productive and innovative.

This well-detailed tidbit is a great way to learn about the many misconceptions associated with humor. It also exposes the many untapped advantages of having a friendly work environment, making it beneficial for leaders, managers, employees, and individuals who want to become better people.

chapters.title: Global humor crisis hits people and organizations
chapters.text:
Our lives change as we mature and join the workforce. Desperate to look professional, most of us prefer to make our encounters sterile, calculated, and official, which is a basic misperception about how to work, handle big issues, behave ourselves, and succeed. Workplaces don't need more professionalism; we need more of ourselves and more personal interaction. A touch of fun can make a transactional and automated encounter more relational and honest. So what's stopping us?

Humor helps us build trust and self-disclosure faster in new relationships, making us happier in existing ones.

Four myths emerged after Dr. Jennifer Aaker and Naomi Bagdonas interviewed over 700 individuals from various sectors and levels.

Business myth

Many people believe that humor has no place in serious work. We fear losing our credibility and dismissal for appearing unserious. This misperception grows as we advance in our occupations. With increased status comes more scrutiny; we feel pressured to show more professionalism and seriousness. Of course, we shouldn't always aim for levity. But we've gone so far the other way that it has become a norm. We need to find a way to balance professionalism with humor.

Failure myth

We've all had people express a profound, crippling dread of failing with humor. They fear the uncomfortable quiet that follows a bad joke, or worse, the discovery that they've accidentally insulted someone. Not every attempt at humor that doesn't succeed is negative, and eliciting laughter doesn't always mean you're humorous.

The being funny myth

Many assume that you must be funny to employ humor and frivolity at work. Yet, simply having a sense of humor is often more important than being humorous. Understanding and valuing humor's role in the workplace can be impactful, especially for leaders. Demonstrating a sense of humor can make a significant difference.

Born with it myth

Too many people think comedy is an innate talent, not a skill. However, humor is a talent, not a hereditary trait.

These myths create misconceptions in our lives, and unless we learn the truth about humor, people will continue to dislike going to work.

chapters.title: Find your humor style and apply it at best value
chapters.text:
Not everyone is equally amusing. Wit comes in different ways, and it is its variety that makes it easy to apply. There are four types of humor:

Stand-up (Aggressive-Expressive)

People in this category are natural performers who aren't afraid to raise a commotion. They excel in front of groups and always deliver the gags. Stand-ups may use cursing, dark humor, pranks, and spontaneous roasts.

Our brains release chemicals that enhance happiness (dopamine), trust (oxytocin), lower stress (cortisol), and create euphoria (endorphins).

The Sweetheart (Affiliative-Subtle)

They prefer subtle humor in a speech or presentation over a quick joke. Sweethearts are kind people who use humor to lift others up and bring people together. If you are optimistic, shun the limelight, and plan your words and their effect, you may be a Sweetheart.

Magnets (Affiliative-Expressive)

They are charming without being obnoxious or rude. Magnets regularly laugh out loud when delivering a joke. A Magnet is a lover of spontaneous humor.

Sniper (Aggressive-Subtle)

Snipers will cross lines for a laugh. They identify their humor as “acquired taste” and say it dryly. They like to watch from the sidelines, quietly preparing their next strike. You have to earn the Sniper's laughter. One-liners are deadly, but Snipers boldly cross boundaries and often go beyond what others anticipate

The collective loss of our sense of humor is a serious problem afflicting people and organizations globally. ~ Dr. Jennifer Aaker

Using humor in business can help us increase:

• Power by elevating others' impressions of our rank and intellect and influencing their actions and decisions.
• Creativity by expanding our vision for novel opportunities and enabling the sharing of bold or unusual ideas.
• Resilience by aiding quicker recovery from difficulties through reducing immediate stress.

chapters.title: Laughter lies in life's little oddities
chapters.text:
To gain a deeper insight into the mechanics and trends of humor, consider analyzing a joke. Picture a dinner party where a guest shows up 30 minutes late for the first course, offering apologies for their tardiness.

“Sorry I’m late. I didn’t want to come.”

Based on a couple of principles, this statement may be moderately amusing to you.

Principle #1: Humor is truth. The guest's statement is a humorous remark since it is more authentic and straightforward than usual. Humor is built on shared facts. So instead of asking what's funny, ask what's truthful.

Principle #2: Humor involves elements of surprise and trickery, since the unexpected often leads to laughter. The Incongruity Resolution Theory states that comedy arises from the discordance between our expectations and reality. People like being shocked; it’s jarring when the joke's setup leads the mind one way, but the punch line abruptly pivots to another.

Your experiences and beliefs influence your viewpoint.

Tapping into your emotions is a good way to find humor from within. Turn your anger into a funny situation, and it will help you and others cope better. You can free your mind of hate and sadness by sharing your emotions in a funny way.

When you think about it, smiling and sharing things that make you happy has more power than you might think. You can do this by looking for things that make you happy and sharing them with others.

Shared laughter doesn’t just create closeness in the moment. It’s equally effective at strengthening relationships over time. ~ Dr. Jennifer Aaker

Contrast and comparison can both be funny. Many comedians excel at highlighting the absurdity of behavior or situations by drawing analogies with other equally ridiculous things. A good analogy shows how you and most people think and feel about two things that are very different from each other.

chapters.title: Words shape our psychology and behavior significantly
chapters.text:
Many of our written statements are barely doing the job of creating an emotional connection; they are only getting information across, but they aren't connecting people with each other. Many believe that corporate communications should be completely free of personality or character. But this is not true. E-mails and memos don't sound like people with emotional abilities wrote them. Humorous words help encourage people to write with feelings and genuine emotions, making it easier for the writer to show their true personality. Companies and organizations need to create safe spaces for people to show off their true selves. To know someone's true intentions, even if it means seeing more of their bad side, is better than pretending to be professional and not telling the truth.

The goal of telling jokes is to be more productive, effective, and less bored in daily settings.

Callbacks highlight a shared history between you and a receiver, converting a single event into a joke. They are especially powerful since they make it simple for another person to react in kind. Recalling a shared experience makes a conversation more pleasant, especially when you recall a time when you and the receiver laughed together. This makes it easier to connect without tension. It also helps you stay professional without being too strict or stiff.

In addition to obtaining callbacks, we must create a positive impression on others. Impressions are formed as a result of having a past. If you have a reputation for being amusing, people will be more ready to pay attention to what you have to say. However, we aren't always able to meet someone face-to-face to make a positive initial impression. Individuals often generate ideas about us before we ever enter a room with them, typically after doing a fast Internet search. Consequently, it's increasingly vital for our online and offline presence to accurately reflect the image we wish to portray to others.

chapters.title: Embracing humor in corporations drastically cuts leadership crises
chapters.text:
Everyone desires a more pleasant, respected boss, yet a leader’s apparent absence of these characteristics contributes to a commitment crisis. During an era where employee turnover has significantly increased compared to the past ten years, costing companies billions, humor's impact on employee morale and hiring emerges as a relevant solution to a widespread, yet understated crisis impacting leaders and organizations around the world.

There are three categories of characters that may contribute positively to the office's humor:

Culture Carriers, who excel in their performance and are both entertaining and highly esteemed in the company, have an innate ability for fun and playfulness. If nurtured and celebrated, they can be a secret asset in creating a lasting, pervasive culture of light-heartedness.

Rebellious Instigators take pleasure in challenging the boundaries. When a culture deviates from its intended path, instigators act as catalysts that spark defiance and playfulness. There will always be individuals in the organization who seem out of place, and if everyone were like them, not much would be accomplished. However, the culture needs some of these individuals because they provide a signal to the rest of us that it is normal to be unusual.

Hidden Gems are dependable, unheralded employees who exhibit surprising abilities. Hidden Gems are high-achieving, diligent people that often go unnoticed due to their inspirational abilities, idiosyncrasies, and interests.

Organizations always have the opportunity to discover special talents in their employees and achieve new levels of performance.

Humor is sometimes perilous; it tests the boundaries of what is acceptable – and not everyone will agree on what is amusing or insulting. If humor becomes taunting, or if the manager makes an employee the object of their humor, the receiver may worry about bullying.

Jokes that depend on stereotypes about race, gender, or sexuality are never appropriate. Power difference alters the interplay between suitable and offensive comedy. Mocking someone of a higher level might establish the humorist as a courageous truth-teller while mocking someone of a lesser status constitutes bullying. If you violate these unwritten standards, apologize immediately.

Did you know? According to a 2019 Gallup poll, over half of all Americans have quit a job to escape their management.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
The alienation of humor in corporate organizations has created a stiff and bland work atmosphere. There's little to no fun in many companies, and this happens because many people, especially leaders, believe that humor has no place in professional corporations. The misconception about humor occurs because not many people truly understand its definition.

In the broader sense, an organization that accepts humor will find it easier to bond and cooperate. Believe it or not, a lighter mood facilitates getting the message across, unlike in an environment where one could cut the tension with a knife. Corporations need to invest more in creati ng a relaxed atmosphere for people to feel comfortable. Productivity increases when people enjoy going to work, and one of the best ways to make people love their work is to create a friendly professional environment. Humor doesn't substitute for comedy, and you don't have to be funny to be humorous. What humor does is create a friendly atmosphere that enables people to be free and open-minded. Humor is about finding the right remarks and making friendly compliments; it helps diffuse a tense situation and gives people more reasons to be agile. A leader can encourage an employee without sounding too dismissive or nonchalant with a humorous remark.

People need to stop seeing professionalism in an opposing way to humor. An environment where people can be themselves is the best place to boost productivity.

Try this

As a leader, pay more attention to giving compliments than scolding your team members. Assist your team without pride, and have a detailed plan on how you can reward your workers for a job done well. Encourage awards and bonuses and make sure you develop a personal relationship with your workers.

Read it till the end?
